was the official [romanization](/wiki/Romanization "Romanization") of [Mandarin](/wiki/Taiwanese_Mandarin "Taiwanese Mandarin") in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") between 2002 and 2008. The system was unofficially used between 2000 and 2002, while a new romanization system for Taiwan was being evaluated for adoption. Taiwan's [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Republic_of_China) "Ministry of Education (Republic of China)") approved the system in 2002,[[1]](#cite_note-taipei_times_2002-07-11-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) but its use was optional.

**Tongyong Pinyin** was the official [romanization](/wiki/Romanization "Romanization") of [Mandarin](/wiki/Taiwanese_Mandarin "Taiwanese Mandarin") in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") between 2002 and 2008. The system was unofficially used between 2000 and 2002, while a new romanization system for Taiwan was being evaluated for adoption. Taiwan's [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Republic_of_China) "Ministry of Education (Republic of China)") approved the system in 2002,[[1]](#cite_note-taipei_times_2002-07-11-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) but its use was optional.

From 1 January 2009, the Ministry of Education began promoting [Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin "Hanyu Pinyin"). Local governments were not able to get financial aid from the central government if they used Tongyong Pinyin-derived romanizations.[[3]](#cite_note-taipei_times_2008-09-18-3)[[4]](#cite_note-china_post_2008-09-18-4) However, Tongyong Pinyin has continued in use for the transliteration of some place names and personal names in Taiwan (Republic of China).[[5]](#cite_note-5) Some of the [romanized](/wiki/Romanization "Romanization") names of the districts, subway stations[[6]](#cite_note-6) and streets[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) in [Kaohsiung](/wiki/Kaohsiung "Kaohsiung"),[[9]](#cite_note-9) [Tainan](/wiki/Tainan "Tainan"),[[10]](#cite_note-govtn-10) [Taichung](/wiki/Taichung "Taichung"),[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-2020taichung-12) [Yunlin County](/wiki/Yunlin_County "Yunlin County")[[13]](#cite_note-13) and other places[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) are derived from Tongyong Pinyin – for example, [Cijin District](/wiki/Cijin_District,_Kaohsiung "Cijin District, Kaohsiung") ([旗津](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%97%97%E6%B4%A5#Chinese_(Taiwan) "wikt:旗津")[區](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8D%80#Chinese_(Taiwan) "wikt:區"), *Cíjin Cyu*).[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tongyong_Pinyin&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/2011-03-20_%E9%AB%98%E9%9B%84%E5%B8%82%E9%B3%B3%E5%B1%B1%E5%8D%80%E5%85%AC%E6%89%80.png/250px-2011-03-20_%E9%AB%98%E9%9B%84%E5%B8%82%E9%B3%B3%E5%B1%B1%E5%8D%80%E5%85%AC%E6%89%80.png)](/wiki/File:2011-03-20_%E9%AB%98%E9%9B%84%E5%B8%82%E9%B3%B3%E5%B1%B1%E5%8D%80%E5%85%AC%E6%89%80.png)

[Fongshan District](/wiki/Fongshan_District "Fongshan District") Office, Kaohsiung City (the spelling 'Fongshan' is derived from the Tongyong Pinyin *Fòngshan*, while the spelling 'Kaohsiung' is derived from Wade–Giles)

The impetus for the invention of Tongyong Pinyin was the need for a standardized romanization system. For decades, the island had employed varying systems, usually simplifications or adaptations of [Wade–Giles](/wiki/Wade%E2%80%93Giles "Wade–Giles").

[Zhuyin](/wiki/Zhuyin "Zhuyin"), a standard phonetic system for language education in Taiwan's schools, does not use the Latin alphabet.

Tongyong Pinyin was introduced in 1998 by Yu Bor-chuan to preserve the strengths of Hanyu Pinyin while eliminating some of the pronunciation difficulties Hanyu presents to international readers, such as difficulties with the letters *q* and *x*. Yu's system was subsequently revised.

Discussion and adoption of Tongyong Pinyin, like many other initiatives in Taiwan, quickly acquired a partisan tone turning on issues of [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_identity "Chinese identity") versus [Taiwanese identity](/wiki/Taiwanese_identity "Taiwanese identity").[[18]](#cite_note-18) Officials who identified most strongly with the nation itself, such as the [Democratic Progressive Party](/wiki/Democratic_Progressive_Party "Democratic Progressive Party") (DPP) and its allied parties, saw no reason to adopt Hanyu Pinyin just because mainland China and the UN had. If Tongyong Pinyin more adequately met the nation's needs, they saw this as ample justification for Taiwan to adopt it.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Officials who identified more strongly with Chinese culture, such as the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") (KMT), saw no reason to introduce a new system unique to Taiwan if Hanyu Pinyin had already gained international acceptance. Each side accused the other of basing its preference on anti-China or pro-China sentiment rather than an objective discussion of community goals.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In early October 2000, the Mandarin Commission of the Ministry of Education proposed to use Tongyong Pinyin as the national standard. Education Minister [Ovid Tzeng](/wiki/Ovid_Tzeng "Ovid Tzeng") submitted a draft of the Taiwanese romanization in late October to the [Executive Yuan](/wiki/Executive_Yuan "Executive Yuan"), but the proposal was rejected. In November 2000, Tzeng unsuccessfully suggested that the government adopt Hanyu Pinyin with some modifications for local dialects. On 10 July 2002, Taiwan's Ministry of Education held a meeting for 27 members. Only 13 attended. Two left early, and since the chairman could not vote, the bill for using Tongyong Pinyin was passed with 10 votes.[[1]](#cite_note-taipei_times_2002-07-11-1)

In August 2002, the government adopted Tongyong Pinyin by an administrative order that local governments had the authority to override within their jurisdiction. In October 2007, with the DPP administration still in power, it was announced that Taiwan would standardize the English transliterations of its Chinese Mandarin place names by the end of the year, after years of confusion from multiple spellings, by using the locally developed Tongyong Pinyin.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In 2008, the Kuomintang won both the [legislative](/wiki/2008_Republic_of_China_legislative_election "2008 Republic of China legislative election") and [presidential](/wiki/2008_Republic_of_China_presidential_election "2008 Republic of China presidential election") elections. In September 2008, it was announced that, at the end of the year, Tongyong Pinyin would be replaced by Hanyu Pinyin as Taiwan's standard. Since 1 January 2009, Hanyu Pinyin has been an official romanization system in Taiwan.[[3]](#cite_note-taipei_times_2008-09-18-3)[[4]](#cite_note-china_post_2008-09-18-4)

However, on 24 August 2020, the [Taichung](/wiki/Taichung "Taichung") City Council decided to use Tongyong Pinyin in the transliterated names of metro stations on the [Green line](/wiki/Green_line_(Taichung_MRT) "Green line (Taichung MRT)").[[12]](#cite_note-2020taichung-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/%E6%A5%A0%E6%A2%93%E8%BB%8A%E7%AB%99.JPG/250px-%E6%A5%A0%E6%A2%93%E8%BB%8A%E7%AB%99.JPG)](/wiki/File:%E6%A5%A0%E6%A2%93%E8%BB%8A%E7%AB%99.JPG)

The sign for [Nanzi Station](/wiki/Nanzi_railway_station "Nanzi railway station") formerly read "Nanzih Station"

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/TRA_Nanzih_Station_by_billy1125_%281%29.jpg/250px-TRA_Nanzih_Station_by_billy1125_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:TRA_Nanzih_Station_by_billy1125_(1).jpg)

The sign was later changed to read "Nanzi Station". The station serves [Nanzih District](/wiki/Nanzih_District "Nanzih District"), Kaohsiung.

## Adoption and use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tongyong_Pinyin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Adoption and use")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Kinmen_-_on_the_road_from_Shuitou_to_Jincheng_-_DSCF9397.JPG/250px-Kinmen_-_on_the_road_from_Shuitou_to_Jincheng_-_DSCF9397.JPG)](/wiki/File:Kinmen_-_on_the_road_from_Shuitou_to_Jincheng_-_DSCF9397.JPG)

Signs using Tongyong Pinyin (*Jhaishan*, *Jhushan* and so on) in [Kinmen](/wiki/Kinmen "Kinmen") in 2012. 金 is misspelled as *jing* (instead of the correct *jin*) in one of the signs

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(December 2010)* |

Tongyong Pinyin was the official romanization system in Taiwan, but its use was voluntary.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The romanization system that one encounters in Taiwan varies according to the government authority that administers the facility. Street signs in most areas use Tongyong Pinyin,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] including the cities of [Kaohsiung](/wiki/Kaohsiung "Kaohsiung"), [Tainan](/wiki/Tainan "Tainan"), and surrounding counties. A contrast could be seen in the two entities that now make up the municipality of [Taichung](/wiki/Taichung "Taichung")–[Taichung County](/wiki/Taichung_County "Taichung County") used Tongyong Pinyin while [Taichung City](/wiki/Taichung_City "Taichung City") has used Hanyu Pinyin since at least 2004. Then-mayor [Ma Ying-jeou](/wiki/Ma_Ying-jeou "Ma Ying-jeou") remained committed to using Hanyu Pinyin as the Romanization standard for Taipei.[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Taipei County](/wiki/Taipei_County "Taipei County") (now New Taipei City) used Tongyong Pinyin, but in [Taipei Metro](/wiki/Taipei_Metro "Taipei Metro") stations, Tongyong Pinyin was given in parentheses after Hanyu Pinyin. Modified Wade–Giles spellings are popularly used for many proper names, especially personal names and businesses.

The political impasse prevented Ministry of Education from being able to replace [Zhuyin](/wiki/Zhuyin "Zhuyin") in teaching pronunciation in elementary school. Zhuyin is widely used to teach Mandarin pronunciation to schoolchildren. Children's books published in Taiwan typically display Zhuyin characters next to Chinese characters in the text.

On 17 September 2008, the Ministry of Education announced that the government standard for romanization would be switched to Hanyu Pinyin nationwide, effective 1 January 2009.[[3]](#cite_note-taipei_times_2008-09-18-3)[[4]](#cite_note-china_post_2008-09-18-4) However, people in Taiwan can freely choose their foreign language names. So although Tongyong Pinyin was effectively scrapped as the romanization standard of Taiwan's central government, many today choose a romanized form of their Chinese character name that is created based on the Tongyong Pinyin, Wade–Giles, or Yale romanization systems.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Today, districts of [Kaohsiung](/wiki/Kaohsiung "Kaohsiung") are transliterated using Tongyong. Districts of [Tainan](/wiki/Tainan "Tainan") are mostly named using Tongyong, with exceptions such as [Xinying](/wiki/Xinying_District "Xinying District").

## Taiwanese language variant

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tongyong_Pinyin&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Taiwanese language variant")]

The Tongyong Pinyin system also exists in a [Taiwanese Hokkien](/wiki/Taiwanese_Hokkien "Taiwanese Hokkien") phonetic symbol version, [Daighi tongiong pingim](/wiki/Daighi_tongiong_pingim "Daighi tongiong pingim"), which lacks *f* but adds *bh*. However, in 2006, the [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Republic_of_China) "Ministry of Education (Republic of China)") rejected the use of Daighi tongiong pingim for Taiwanese Hokkien and preferred the [Taiwanese Romanization System](/wiki/Taiwanese_Romanization_System "Taiwanese Romanization System").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tongyong_Pinyin&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Features")]

### Spelling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tongyong_Pinyin&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Spelling")]

Some notable features of Tongyong Pinyin are:

* The first tone is unmarked.
* Hanyu Pinyin's *zh-* becomes *jh-* ([Wade–Giles](/wiki/Wade%E2%80%93Giles "Wade–Giles") uses *ch-*).
* Hanyu Pinyin's *x-* and *q-* are not used in Tongyong Pinyin and become *s-* and *c-* (Wade–Giles uses *hs-* and *ch'-*).
* The Hanyu Pinyin *-i* (not represented in Zhuyin) known as the empty rhyme (空韻), are shown as *-ih* (somewhat like Wade–Giles): those in Hanyu Pinyin as *zi* (資), *ci* (慈), *si* (思), *zhi* (知), *chi* (吃), *shi* (詩), and *ri* (日) all end in *-ih* in Tongyong Pinyin.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/%E8%87%BA%E5%8D%97%E5%B8%82%E5%AD%B8%E7%94%B2%E5%9C%8B%E4%B8%AD.JPG/250px-%E8%87%BA%E5%8D%97%E5%B8%82%E5%AD%B8%E7%94%B2%E5%9C%8B%E4%B8%AD.JPG)](/wiki/File:%E8%87%BA%E5%8D%97%E5%B8%82%E5%AD%B8%E7%94%B2%E5%9C%8B%E4%B8%AD.JPG)

Syuejia/SyueJia Junior High School, [Syuejia District, Tainan](/wiki/Syuejia_District,_Tainan "Syuejia District, Tainan"), Taiwan (the spelling 'Syuejia' is derived from the Tongyong Pinyin *Syuéjiǎ*.)

* *ü* used in Hanyu Pinyin (written *u* after *j*, *q* and *x*) is replaced by *yu*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/%E5%8F%B0%E4%B8%AD%E6%8D%B7%E9%81%8B2018.01-wiki.jpg/250px-%E5%8F%B0%E4%B8%AD%E6%8D%B7%E9%81%8B2018.01-wiki.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E5%8F%B0%E4%B8%AD%E6%8D%B7%E9%81%8B2018.01-wiki.jpg)

[Taichung MRT](/wiki/Taichung_MRT "Taichung MRT") includes a station at Fongle Park (from Tongyong Pinyin *Fonglè*)

* *-eng* becomes *ong* after *f-* and *w-* (奉、瓮).
* *wen* (溫) becomes *wun*.
* *-iong* becomes *yong*: *syong* instead of pinyin *xiong* (兇) (cf. *-iang* remains unchanged: *siang*).
* Unlike in Wade–Giles and Hanyu Pinyin, *-iu* and *-ui* (*liu* [六] and *gui* [鬼]), contractions can be written out in full as *-iou* and *-uei*. According to the [Ministry of the Interior](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Interior "Ministry of the Interior"), in romanizations of names of places that is at township-level or below township-level, the letters must be written in full.

### Punctuation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tongyong_Pinyin&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Punctuation")]

* Tongyong syllables in the same word (except [placenames](/wiki/Placename "Placename")) are to be separated by [hyphens](/wiki/Hyphen "Hyphen"), like Wade–Giles, but in the Ministry of the Interior's romanizations, placenames have no spaces between the syllables.
* Tongyong uses [tone](/wiki/Tone_(tonal_language) "Tone (tonal language)") closer to those used in [Zhuyin](/wiki/Zhuyin "Zhuyin"), than Hanyu Pinyin: it has no mark for the first tone, but uses a small ring for the neutral tone (optional on computers).

### Shared features with Hanyu Pinyin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tongyong_Pinyin&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Shared features with Hanyu Pinyin")]

If tone is ignored, 19.47% of Tongyong Pinyin [syllables](/wiki/Syllable "Syllable") are spelled differently to those of Hanyu Pinyin. The difference widens when syllables are measured according to average frequency of use in everyday life to a 48.84% difference in spellings.[[26]](#cite_note-ty_hy_compare-26) In two cases (*si* and *ci*) the same Latin spelling denotes different syllables depending on the transcription system.

## Arguments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tongyong_Pinyin&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Arguments")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section **is written like a debate**. Please help [rewrite it](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Tongyong_Pinyin&action=edit) in a [neutral](/wiki/Wikipedia:NPOV "Wikipedia:NPOV"), [encyclopedic](/wiki/Wikipedia:Encyclopedic_style "Wikipedia:Encyclopedic style") style. *(May 2022)* |

The prevalence of Hanyu Pinyin as an established system weighs at least as heavily on the debate over Tongyong Pinyin as any feature of the system itself. There are many arguments for the use of each. Some factors cited in support of Tongyong Pinyin include the following.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Road_name_sign_of_Lane_782%2C_Jyunsiao_Road%2C_Nanzih_20130301.jpg/250px-Road_name_sign_of_Lane_782%2C_Jyunsiao_Road%2C_Nanzih_20130301.jpg)](/wiki/File:Road_name_sign_of_Lane_782,_Jyunsiao_Road,_Nanzih_20130301.jpg)

Road sign in [Nanzih District](/wiki/Nanzih_District "Nanzih District"), Kaohsiung in which [軍校](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%BB%8D%E6%A0%A1#Chinese_(Taiwan) "wikt:軍校")[路](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%B7%AF#Chinese_(Taiwan) "wikt:路") (Hanyu Pinyin: *jūnxiào lù*) is written as 'Jyunsiao Rd.', based on the Tongyong Pinyin form *jyunsiào lù*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/NTU_Sin_Sheng_Building_stand_20200316.jpg/250px-NTU_Sin_Sheng_Building_stand_20200316.jpg)](/wiki/File:NTU_Sin_Sheng_Building_stand_20200316.jpg)

Sign at [National Taiwan University](/wiki/National_Taiwan_University "National Taiwan University") in [Taipei](/wiki/Taipei "Taipei"), [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") in which 新生大樓 ([Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin "Hanyu Pinyin"): *xīnshēng dàlóu*) is written as 'Sin Sheng Building', based on the Tongyong Pinyin form *sinsheng dàlóu*.

* Tongyong Pinyin is business-friendly because of the ease it offers in pronunciation. Visitors to Taiwan can thus more easily describe and find place names, personal names, businesses and locales.
* Tongyong Pinyin requires no more special accommodation in international correspondence than the difference in Chinese characters (simplified vs. traditional) already requires.
* Tongyong strikes a balance between the need for internationalization and Taiwan's local needs.[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* Tongyong Pinyin would not supplant Hanyu Pinyin in Taiwan, as Hanyu Pinyin is rarely encountered outside the Taipei area anyway and has never been in common use. Tongyong Pinyin is intended to supplant the many variants of Wade–Giles that remain the dominant form of romanization encountered in Taiwan. No one questions the superiority of Tongyong Pinyin to Wade–Giles and the benefit to be gained from the change.
* Tongyong does not force its exclusive use on those who have already studied Hanyu Pinyin. One can use any system to render characters while one types or formats documents in Mandarin. Computers and electronic devices in Taiwan already offer Hanyu Pinyin and MPS keyboards as options. Transitions between romanized forms are also easily achieved if needed.
* Romanization is most useful to individuals who lack training in Mandarin but encounter names and terms in press reports and literature. Students of Mandarin gain literacy in Chinese characters and drop romanization systems of any kind.

Some argue against the system. The reasons they cite include:

* The standard romanization system of [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"), the [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization"), and the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") is Hanyu Pinyin.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* Tongyong Pinyin creates a third form of spelling/transliteration, adding complexity. For example, "Qing dynasty" (via Hanyu Pinyin) and "Ch'ing dynasty" (via Wade–Giles) is spelled as "Cing dynasty" (via Tongyong Pinyin). Persons doing research on this time period thus would need to know that all three terms in fact refer to the same dynasty.

## Comparison with other orthographies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tongyong_Pinyin&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Comparison with other orthographies")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/China_hanzi.png/250px-China_hanzi.png)](/wiki/File:China_hanzi.png)

The word for 'China', written in [Hanyu Pinyin](/wiki/Hanyu_Pinyin "Hanyu Pinyin"), Tongyong Pinyin, and Chinese characters (traditional and simplified)

The differences between Tongyong Pinyin and Hanyu Pinyin[[29]](#cite_note-29) are relatively straightforward:

* The palatalized consonants are written *j, c, s* rather than *j, q, x*.
* The retroflex consonants are *jh, ch, sh* rather than *zh, ch, sh*.
* The "buzzing" vowels are written *ih (shih, sih)* rather than *i (shi, si)*.
* *Yu* and *yong* are still spelled with a 'y' even after a consonant *(nyu, *jyong*),* rather than as *ü, u,* or *iong*.
* *You* and *wei* are written *iou* and *uei* after a consonant *(diou, duei),* rather than contracted to *iu* and *ui*.
* *Eng* is written labialized *ong* after the labial consonants *f, w (fong, wong),* but *weng/wong* contracts to *ong* after another consonant in both systems.
* *Wen* becomes *wun*.
* Neutral tone is marked, but not first tone.
